prompt_text: Best automation platforms for self-host workflow automation with full data control and no per-task pricing limits
response:
For **self-hosted workflow automation** with **full data control** and **no per-task pricing**, the strongest options are **n8n**, **Activepieces**, **Windmill**, **Apache Airflow**, and **Camunda**. Self-hosting keeps automations and data on your own infrastructure, and these tools are commonly recommended as open-source or self-hostable alternatives that avoid per-task fees[1][3][5].

- **n8n** — Best fit if you want a broad automation platform with many integrations and self-host deployment; it is listed among top open-source workflow automation tools[5].
- **Activepieces** — Good choice for low-code automation with an open-source, self-hosted model; it is explicitly highlighted as open-source and self-hosted[2][3][5].
- **Windmill** — Strong option for technical teams that want scripts, internal tools, and workflow automation in one self-hosted system; it appears in open-source workflow tool roundups[5].
- **Apache Airflow** — Best for data pipelines and scheduled orchestrations rather than general business automation; it is a standard open-source workflow engine in these lists[3][5].
- **Camunda** — Better for BPM/process-heavy workflows where you need formal process modeling and enterprise controls; it is included among top open-source workflow automation tools[3].
- **Imixs-Workflow / Bonitasoft / CloudSlang** — More specialized alternatives if your use case is business process automation or engine-style orchestration[3].

If your priority is **“no per-task pricing limits”**, the practical meaning is usually **self-hosted open source** or a **self-managed server license**, because cloud automation platforms often price by task execution volume[1]. Among these, **n8n** and **Activepieces** are usually the most accessible starting points for general-purpose automation, while **Airflow** and **Camunda** are better when your workflows are more technical or process-centric[3][5].

If you want, I can narrow this to the **best 3 for non-technical users**, **best 3 for developers**, or a **comparison table by features, ease of self-hosting, and integration depth**.
